text: In this video we present the case of sixty nine years old man, who developed a left ureteral stricture in presence of a nine millimeters stone. Preliminary ureteroscopy confirmed a tight ureteral stricture. A nephrostomy tub was placed and a combined pyelography showed a length of about three centimeters of the stricture.

The surgeon performs a robot-assisted ureteroplasty using a buccal mucosa graft. The ureter is prepared with translumination and Firefly tool guidance, and the stricture is characterized using flexible ureteroscopy and ICG injection techniques. The ureteral wall is incised, preserving the posterior plate, and the stone is removed. The buccal mucosa graft is harvested from the cheek and sutured with four half sutures to prevent curling. A ureteral stent is placed using an endoscope.
The surgeon opens Girota's fascia, utilizing transillumination via a ureteroscope to locate and guide the dissection of the left ureter.

The surgeon uses transnephrostomic ICG injection and endoscopic transillumination to identify the proximal and distal ends of the ureteral stricture. The proximal end is incised, revealing the guide wire. The distal end is also incised, followed by a longitudinal incision along the anterior wall of the stricture, while preserving the posterior plate.

The surgeon progressively removes the stone impacted in the ureteral mucosa. A caliper is used to measure the stricture. A flexible ureteroscopy is performed to check for residual stones. The buccal mucosa graft, harvested from the patient's cheek, is sutured to the ureter using a four-zero absorbable suture in a running fashion, with four half sutures to prevent curling.

An omentum flap is created and wrapped around the patch to support vascularization. Teseal is applied to protect the patch and the errator. The surgical site is shown with the omentum securely enveloping the patch.
